title: William Hoffman (United States Army)

(1807–1884) was a 19th-century officer in the [United States Army](/wiki/United_States_Army "United States Army"). The West Point graduate was involved in the [Black Hawk War](/wiki/Black_Hawk_War "Black Hawk War"), [Seminole Wars](/wiki/Seminole_Wars "Seminole Wars"), [Mexican–American War](/wiki/Mexican%E2%80%93American_War "Mexican–American War") and the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War"). During the Civil War he served as the Commissary-General of Prisoners and set policy for the treatment and release of prisoners.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

**William Hoffman** (1807–1884) was a 19th-century officer in the [United States Army](/wiki/United_States_Army "United States Army"). The West Point graduate was involved in the [Black Hawk War](/wiki/Black_Hawk_War "Black Hawk War"), [Seminole Wars](/wiki/Seminole_Wars "Seminole Wars"), [Mexican–American War](/wiki/Mexican%E2%80%93American_War "Mexican–American War") and the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War"). During the Civil War he served as the Commissary-General of Prisoners and set policy for the treatment and release of prisoners.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

## Biography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=William_Hoffman_(United_States_Army)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Biography")]

### Early life and education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=William_Hoffman_(United_States_Army)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early life and education")]

William Hoffman Jr. was born in New York. He was the son of [Lieutenant Colonel](/wiki/Lieutenant_Colonel_(United_States) "Lieutenant Colonel (United States)") William Hoffman Sr., who fought in the [War of 1812](/wiki/War_of_1812 "War of 1812"). He graduated from the [United States Military Academy](/wiki/United_States_Military_Academy "United States Military Academy") at [West Point, New York](/wiki/West_Point,_New_York "West Point, New York"), and was commissioned a [2nd Lieutenant](/wiki/2nd_Lieutenant "2nd Lieutenant") in the [6th U.S. Infantry](/wiki/6th_Infantry_Regiment_(United_States) "6th Infantry Regiment (United States)") on July 1, 1829. He was a classmate of [Robert E. Lee](/wiki/Robert_E._Lee "Robert E. Lee").[[2]](#cite_note-Heidler-2)

### Early military career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=William_Hoffman_(United_States_Army)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early military career")]

Hoffman's early military career was on the frontier. Between 1829 and 1836 he was stationed at either [Jefferson Barracks](/wiki/Jefferson_Barracks_Military_Post "Jefferson Barracks Military Post") in [Missouri](/wiki/Missouri "Missouri") or [Fort Leavenworth](/wiki/Fort_Leavenworth "Fort Leavenworth") in [Kansas](/wiki/Kansas "Kansas").[[3]](#cite_note-Aztec-3) In 1832 he fought in the [Black Hawk War](/wiki/Black_Hawk_War "Black Hawk War"). On November 16, 1836, he was promoted to [1st Lieutenant](/wiki/1st_Lieutenant "1st Lieutenant"), 6th U.S. Infantry. Hoffman then moved south to [Fort Jesup](/wiki/Fort_Jesup "Fort Jesup") and Camp Sabine in [Louisiana](/wiki/Louisiana "Louisiana") before fighting in the [Second Seminole War](/wiki/Second_Seminole_War "Second Seminole War") from 1837 to 1842. On February 1, 1838, he was promoted to [captain](/wiki/Captain_(United_States_O-3) "Captain (United States O-3)"), 6th U.S. Infantry.[[3]](#cite_note-Aztec-3) In 1842 he served in the Recruiting Service before being posted at [Fort Smith](/wiki/Fort_Smith_National_Historic_Site "Fort Smith National Historic Site") in [Arkansas](/wiki/Arkansas "Arkansas"). In 1846 he mustered volunteers into the military before his engagement in the [Mexican–American War](/wiki/Mexican%E2%80%93American_War "Mexican–American War").

During the war with [Mexico](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico") between 1846 and 1847, Hoffman was engaged in the march through [Chihuahua](/wiki/Chihuahua_(state) "Chihuahua (state)"), the [Siege of Vera Cruz](/wiki/Siege_of_Vera_Cruz "Siege of Vera Cruz"), the [Battle of Cerro Gordo](/wiki/Battle_of_Cerro_Gordo "Battle of Cerro Gordo"), a skirmish at Amazoque, the Capture of [San Antonio](/wiki/San_Antonio "San Antonio"), the [Battle of Contreras](/wiki/Battle_of_Contreras "Battle of Contreras") and the [Battle of Churubusco](/wiki/Battle_of_Churubusco "Battle of Churubusco"), where he was wounded.[[2]](#cite_note-Heidler-2) On August 20, 1847, Hoffman was [brevetted](/wiki/Brevet_(military) "Brevet (military)") to [major](/wiki/Major_(United_States) "Major (United States)") for gallant and meritorious conduct in the Battles of Contreras and Churubusco.[[3]](#cite_note-Aztec-3) He was also involved in the [Battle of Molino del Rey](/wiki/Battle_of_Molino_del_Rey "Battle of Molino del Rey"), the [Battle of Chapultepec](/wiki/Battle_of_Chapultepec "Battle of Chapultepec"), and the Assault and Capture of [Mexico City](/wiki/Battle_of_Mexico_City "Battle of Mexico City"). He was brevetted a [lieutenant colonel](/wiki/Lieutenant_colonel_(United_States) "Lieutenant colonel (United States)") on September 8, 1847, for his gallant and meritorious conduct in the Battle of Molino del Rey.[[3]](#cite_note-Aztec-3)

Between the war with Mexico and the Civil War Hoffman served on recruiting duty for two years before returning to the frontier. There he was garrisoned again at Fort Leavenworth and was promoted to major, [5th U.S. Infantry](/wiki/5th_Infantry_Regiment_(United_States) "5th Infantry Regiment (United States)"), on April 15, 1851. He returned to Jefferson Barracks in 1852 and was transferred back to the 6th U.S. Infantry the same year. From 1852 to 1854 he was garrisoned at [Newport Barracks](/wiki/Newport_Barracks "Newport Barracks") in [Kentucky](/wiki/Kentucky "Kentucky"). He returned to the frontier in 1854 with his posting at [Fort Laramie](/wiki/Fort_Laramie "Fort Laramie") in the [Dakota Territory](/wiki/Dakota_Territory "Dakota Territory"). He was involved in the Sioux Expedition of 1855, the [Utah Expedition](/wiki/Utah_War "Utah War") of 1858 and the March to California the same year. Hoffman relocated to California for frontier duty in the [Mojave Country](/wiki/Mohave_people "Mohave people") and [Benicia, California](/wiki/Benicia,_California "Benicia, California"). On October 17, 1860, he was promoted to lieutenant colonel, [8th U.S. Infantry](/wiki/8th_Infantry_Regiment_(United_States) "8th Infantry Regiment (United States)"), and was transferred to San Antonio, Texas.[[3]](#cite_note-Aztec-3)

### Civil War

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=William_Hoffman_(United_States_Army)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Civil War")]

Hoffman was in San Antonio at the start of the American Civil War. He was taken as a [prisoner of war](/wiki/Prisoner_of_war "Prisoner of war") by the [Confederates](/wiki/Confederate_States_Army "Confederate States Army") when [Brigadier General](/wiki/Brigadier_General_(United_States) "Brigadier General (United States)") [David E. Twiggs](/wiki/David_E._Twiggs "David E. Twiggs") surrendered the Union command on February 18, 1861. On April 25, 1862, he was promoted to [colonel](/wiki/Colonel_(United_States) "Colonel (United States)"), [3rd U.S. Infantry](/wiki/3rd_US_Infantry_Regiment_(The_Old_Guard) "3rd US Infantry Regiment (The Old Guard)"), while still a prisoner of war, and was released in a prisoner exchange on August 27, 1862.

He was reassigned to Washington, D.C. where that year he became Commissary-General of Prisoners. The Office of the Commissary-General of Prisoners had been organized on October 7, 1861, under the Quartermaster General's Department.[[4]](#cite_note-Eicher-4) It was made directly responsible to the [Secretary of War](/wiki/Secretary_of_War "Secretary of War") on June 17, 1862, so when Hoffman took the position, he had access to the top of the military and political structure.

On November 11, 1864, the office was divided into an Eastern and Western Branch. Hoffman, who had been in charge of the whole office up to that date, was placed in charge of the Eastern Branch. On February 1, 1865, it was restructured as a single unit, and Hoffman was placed in charge overall again.[[4]](#cite_note-Eicher-4)

Over 104,000 Confederate soldiers deserted the army during the war, with a third of them surrendering to the Union army.[[2]](#cite_note-Heidler-2) In addition, the army captured thousands of soldiers, whom they held as prisoners of war, transferring them away from the fronts to camps set up to house them. The two governments negotiated a prisoner exchange, which operated from 1862 and 1863 before it broke down for a variety of reasons.[[2]](#cite_note-Heidler-2)

The Union was not prepared to handle the number of prisoners it took in and scrambled to set up facilities. On both sides, the prison camps were overcrowded, suffered food shortages and poor sanitation, and were plagued with infectious disease. In 1862 some of the Confederate prisoners of war refused to be exchanged, saying they would not return to the South.[[2]](#cite_note-Heidler-2)

Hoffman, working with President [Abraham Lincoln](/wiki/Abraham_Lincoln "Abraham Lincoln") and War Secretary [Edwin Stanton](/wiki/Edwin_Stanton "Edwin Stanton"), developed a procedure whereby Confederate prisoners of war and deserters could swear allegiance to the Union to gain their release. Release requests from Union officials, Confederate soldiers, and Southern family members came to Hoffman's office for review and evaluation. Hoffman believed deserters, because they had already renounced their military obligation to the Confederacy, provided an opportunity to "reconstruct" the rebel soldiers and undermine the Confederate war effort.[[2]](#cite_note-Heidler-2) Military field commanders could administer the oath of allegiance to deserters if they could verify their stories and be assured they were not spies. He believed that prisoners of war presented a problem, as they could return to their units or act as spies. They could only be released after Hoffman's staff reviewed each case individually and the release was approved by Secretary of War Stanton.[[2]](#cite_note-Heidler-2)

To encourage desertion, the Union started to offer incentives to Confederate soldiers, such as transportation home. Hoffman narrowed the conditions for Confederates to take the oath of allegiance, and the number of prisoners released decreased dramatically.[[2]](#cite_note-Heidler-2)

On October 7, 1864, Hoffman was brevetted to brigadier general for faithful and meritorious services during the Rebellion, and then brevetted to [major general](/wiki/Major_general_(United_States) "Major general (United States)") on March 13, 1865, for faithful, meritorious and distinguished services as Commissary-General of Prisoners during the Rebellion. He served in the post of Commissary-General until November 3, 1865.

### Later career and death

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=William_Hoffman_(United_States_Army)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Later career and death")]

After the Civil War, Hoffman was placed in command of regiments at [St. Louis, Missouri](/wiki/St._Louis,_Missouri "St. Louis, Missouri"), from 1865 to 1866 and Fort Leavenworth from 1866 to 1868.[[3]](#cite_note-Aztec-3) He was on leave from March 6, 1868, to April 26, 1869, when he became the Superintendent of the General Recruiting Service. He retired from the army on May 1, 1870, as a colonel.[[2]](#cite_note-Heidler-2) Hoffman retired to [Rock Island, Illinois](/wiki/Rock_Island,_Illinois "Rock Island, Illinois"), where he married his second wife. He and his wife Mary started a school for girls there.[[5]](#cite_note-Speer-5) Hoffman died in 1884.
